subject: Chemistry
solution:
Zn is usually not considered as a transition element as the d-orbital electrons do not take part in reactions. This is why Zn exhibit only +2  oxidation state.
But since, it is placed in the d-block along with other transition metals, it is technically a d-block element and a transition metal. Zinc has the lowest enthalpy of atomization among the first row transition metals.

Elements V Cu Zn Fe
Enthalpy of atomization 515 KJ/mol 339 KJ/mol 216 KJ/mol 416 KJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The electronic configuration of Cr is $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$ and Mn is $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^2$.

For the first ionization enthalpy, the electron is removed from the $4s$ orbital. Mn has a fully filled $4s^2$ configuration and a higher effective nuclear charge compared to Cr. Thus, the first ionization enthalpy of Mn is greater than that of Cr ($\Delta_i H_1 :$ Mn $>$ Cr). This makes statement C correct.

After the first ionization, the electronic configurations become:
$Cr^+ : [Ar] 3d^5$
$Mn^+ : [Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$

For the second ionization enthalpy, the electron is removed from the highly stable half-filled $3d^5$ subshell in $Cr^+$, whereas in $Mn^+$, it is removed from the $4s^1$ orbital. Removing an electron from a stable half-filled configuration requires significantly more energy. Thus, the second ionization enthalpy of Cr is greater than that of Mn ($\Delta_i H_2 :$ Cr $>$ Mn). This makes statement B correct.

Therefore, the correct statements are B and C.

Answer: B and C only

subject: Chemistry
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
The Lanthanides 4f-and Actinides 5f- inner transition series of elements are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table. Reason: (i) This prevents the undue expansion of the periodic table i.e., maintains its structure and (ii) also because their properties are different from other elements.

The main reason that s-block elements never occur in free state/nature is that these are alkali and alkali earth metals which are highly reactive. The high reactivity comes from their low ionization energy.
They have either one or two electrons in their outermost orbit so they tend to form stable products as soon as they get another element wanting electrons. Thus, s-block elements are never found in a free state in nature.
They are always found in formed compounds, such as silicates, carbonates, oxides, etc. As the elements are highly reactive, they easily accept elements or compounds.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
In d-block elements, the stability of higher oxidation states increases down a group. Therefore, $Mo(VI)$ and $W(VI)$ are more stable than $Cr(VI)$. Statement A is incorrect.

The most stable oxidation state for lanthanoids is $+3$. Ions like $Ce^{4+}$ and $Tb^{4+}$ tend to gain an electron to achieve the $+3$ state, acting as oxidants. Conversely, $Eu^{2+}$ and $Yb^{2+}$ tend to lose an electron to achieve the $+3$ state, acting as reductants. Statement B is correct.

The electronic configuration of $Am$ is $[Rn] 5f^{7} 7s^{2}$, which has $7$ unpaired electrons. The electronic configuration of $Cm$ is $[Rn] 5f^{7} 6d^{1} 7s^{2}$, which has $8$ unpaired electrons ($7$ in $5f$ and $1$ in $6d$). Statement C is incorrect.

The $5f$ electrons have a poorer shielding effect compared to $4f$ electrons. As a result, the effective nuclear charge increases more significantly across the actinoid series, leading to a greater actinoid contraction from element to element than the lanthanoid contraction. Statement D is correct.

Thus, only statements B and D are correct.

Answer: B and D Only

Hence, due to more reduction potential in Eu as compared to Yb, it can concluded that Eu2+ is more stable than Yb2+.

solution:
$\underset{(\mathrm{O}. \mathrm{A})}{\stackrel{+7}{\mathrm{KMnO}}} \xrightarrow{\text { Acidic medium }} \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$
X is difference in oxidation state.
$6 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}+\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Fe}_3\left(\mathrm{OH}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}\right)_6\right]^{\oplus}+2 \mathrm{H}^{\oplus}$
$\left[\mathrm{Fe}_3(\mathrm{OH})_2\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}\right)_6\right]^{\oplus} \text{(Brown red ppt)} +4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_2\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}\right]+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\right.$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The electronic configurations of the given elements are:

Eu (63): [Xe] $4f^7 6s^2$
Gd (64): [Xe] $4f^7 5d^1 6s^2$
Dy (66): [Xe] $4f^{10} 6s^2$
Ho (67): [Xe] $4f^{11} 6s^2$
Tm (69): [Xe] $4f^{13} 6s^2$
Yb (70): [Xe] $4f^{14} 6s^2$
Lu (71): [Xe] $4f^{14} 5d^1 6s^2$
Hf (72): [Xe] $4f^{14} 5d^2 6s^2$

From the above configurations:
Eu and Gd both have 7 electrons in their $4f$ orbitals.
Yb and Hf both have 14 electrons in their $4f$ orbitals.
Dy and Ho have 10 and 11 electrons respectively in their $4f$ orbitals.
Lu and Tm have 14 and 13 electrons respectively in their $4f$ orbitals.

Thus, pairs A (Eu and Gd) and C (Yb and Hf) have the same number of electrons in their respective $4f$ orbitals.

Answer: A and C Only

subject: Chemistry
solution:
This KMnO4 is a powerful oxidizing agent in neutral, acidic, and alkaline medium. The H2O2 is also an oxidizing agent but in the reaction of KMnO4 with the H2O2. The H2O2 loses its electron and acts as a reducing agent. In an acidic medium, theMnO4- ions involve the addition of 5 electrons and the two peroxide ions give out two electrons. In dilute alkaline medium or in neutral medium MnO4- ions involve the addition of 3 electrons. The reaction in basic medium is given below.

2KMnO4+3H2O2⟶basic medium2MnO2+4+3O2+2H2O+2KOH

The oxidation state of Mn in MnO2 is +4.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The oxidation state or oxidation number is defined as the loss or gain of electrons from an atom, molecule, or compound.

The reaction of potassium dichromate, potassium chloride and sulphuric acid (conc.)is as follows,

K2Cr2O7s+4KCls+6H2SO4 (conc.)

→2CrO2Cl2g+6KHSO4+3H2O

This reaction takes place in chromyl chloride test.

The Chromyl chloride is a neutral molecule as it does not have any charge on it.

Here, oxidation state of Cr  in CrO2Cl2 is +6.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Statement A: Vanadium pentoxide ($\text{V}_2\text{O}_5$) is used as a catalyst in the manufacture of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ by the Contact process. $\text{V}_2\text{O}_5$ is an amphoteric oxide, not a basic oxide. Thus, statement A is incorrect.

Statement B: The transition metal halide used in Ziegler-Natta polymerization is $\text{TiCl}_4$. In $\text{TiCl}_4$, titanium is in the $+4$ oxidation state with a $3\text{d}^0$ electronic configuration. The number of unpaired electrons is zero, so its spin-only magnetic moment is $0$ BM. Thus, statement B is incorrect.

Statement C: The p-block metal compound used in Ziegler-Natta polymerization is triethylaluminium, $\text{Al}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_3$. Aluminium is a p-block element and is in the $+3$ oxidation state. Thus, statement C is correct.

Statement D: The metal halide employed in the Wacker process is $\text{PdCl}_2$. Palladium (Pd) has the electronic configuration $[\text{Kr}] 4\text{d}^{10}$. In $\text{PdCl}_2$, Pd is in the $+2$ oxidation state, giving it a $4\text{d}^8$ configuration. The number of electrons in the outermost d-orbital is $8$. Thus, statement D is correct.

Therefore, only statements C and D are correct.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Charge transfer from  O 2 - to empty d -orbitals of metal ion (Mn 7+ ).
The colour in KMnO 4 arises from an electronic transition. In the permanganate ion (MnO 4 − ), manganese is in the +7 oxidation state, so it has no d electrons. Photons promote an electron from the highest energy molecular orbital to an empty d -orbital on the manganese. When a photon of light is absorbed, this charge transfer takes place from L to M.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The highest manganese fluoride is $MnF_4$, where the oxidation state of Mn is $+4$.

The highest manganese oxide is $Mn_2O_7$, where the oxidation state of Mn is $+7$.

The difference in oxidation states is $|x| = |7 - 4| = 3$.

The number of unpaired electrons in the given ions are:
$Sc^{3+}$: $[Ar] 3d^0 \Rightarrow 0$ unpaired electrons
$Zn^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^{10} \Rightarrow 0$ unpaired electrons
$V^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^3 \Rightarrow 3$ unpaired electrons
$Fe^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^6 \Rightarrow 4$ unpaired electrons
$Co^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^7 \Rightarrow 3$ unpaired electrons

Thus, $V^{2+}$ (C) and $Co^{2+}$ (E) have $3$ unpaired electrons.

Answer: C and E Only

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Analyzing each statement:

Statement A: In Mn₂O₇, using oxidation state calculation: 2(Mn) + 7(O⁻²) = 0, so Mn oxidation state = +7. This is indeed the highest oxidation state for Mn. TRUE.

Statement B: Oxygen stabilizes Mn in +7 oxidation state through multiple bonding (Mn=O double bonds and bridging), reducing electron density effectively. TRUE.

Statement C: Mn₂O₇ is primarily a covalent oxide with significant Mn-O covalent bonding character. The oxide is not ionic in nature. FALSE.

Statement D: The structure of Mn₂O₇ contains bridged oxygen atoms (Mn-O-Mn linkages) along with terminal Mn=O bonds. TRUE.

Statements A, B, and D are correct while C is incorrect. The answer is option (1): A, B and D Only.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The common oxidation state of lanthanide is +3. The lanthanide ions having +2 oxidation state undergo oxidation readily, hence they act as reducing agents. The lanthanide ions having  +4 oxidation state undergo reduction readily, hence they act as oxidising agents.

Hence, Ce+4 and Tb+4 act as oxidising agent
